data.title: Tesco: Opportunity Captured, Upside Fading
data.description:
So, what did you think of the 'Christmas Dip'?

I hope you did!

Back in January I told you that Tesco's (TSCDY) post-Christmas trading update sell-off had gone too far.

Instead of focusing on share gains driven by pricing investments (U.K. share up 31 bp to 29.4% post-Christmas and Aldi Price Match), the market only looked at the slowdown in same-store sales, from 3.9% in Q3 FY 2026 to 3.2% over Christmas, with the pressure coming from the mix rather than traffic, plus some weakness at Booker.

I was sitting on a 'Hold' position here but moved to 'Buy' once the stock hit ~$16.

With a price target of $19 to $22 per share, the upside would be at least 18% with a mid-single-digit shareholder yield.

In other words, you were looking at a defensive compounder with approximately 20% total return potential.

Not bad, don't you think?

Time passed, and it looks like that call played out.

The stock is up just over 18%, while the S&P is only up 2.6% over the same period. And, most importantly, Tesco is already trading above the lower end of my price target.

What to do now?

Well, before I give you some perspectives for FY 2027 for Tesco, I want to take a peek at the last quarter before that.

The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly

Sorry, this piece probably won't live up to a Sergio Leone film, but I like the idea.

And, if we're going to separate 'The Good' here, we need to spend some time talking about some things Tesco did throughout FY 2026:

  1. Managed to expand its market share in the UK to ~28.5%, maintaining its market leadership through price investments (£2.2 billion in the last four years) even under pressure from hard-discounters like Aldi.
  2. Expanded the line of private labels and fresh products (this includes the entire story of the 'Finest' and 'Chef's Collection' lines). That's an edge, especially against grocers that operate with a narrower SKU mix.
  3. Operates Whoosh (~51% year-over-year growth), maintaining ultra-fast in-house delivery services, protecting them from Uber Eats and Deliveroo.

It's clear they're still pushing back against the 'Aldi threat,' just like regional names such as Weis Markets (WMK) and Village Super Market (VLGEA) in the U.S. (private label, fresh, even some in-store dining).

But at the same time, Tesco isn't backing off on price.

They have scale and aren't afraid to use it, even if it costs a chunk of margins.

Another positive point here is that this boost in both product mix and traffic is allowing them to expand sales in the same stores.

This is also interesting, since Tesco doesn't have a specific hard-discount banner like Metro (MRU:CA) and Loblaw (L:CA).

And yet they are seeing gains in traffic by running a hybrid supermarket model (Aldi Price Match, for example, replicates Aldi prices on some items, maintaining visits from more price-conscious shoppers).

For the whole of FY 2026, for example, same-store sales expanded by 3.5%, with a further boost (~5%) in England and Ireland. This allowed them to maintain a low- to mid-single-digit pace in sales growth.

Here's a peek at revenue (in pounds sterling) and year-over-year growth:

The cherry on top here is that FCF also increased, even with slightly tighter margins than last year (I'll talk about that soon).

OCF increased by almost £1 billion to £3.9 billion. Of course, there was a 'small but significant' boost from operating assets. But even if we consider only net income + D&A (~£3.7 billion), adjusted OCF actually increased by about 8.8%.

Capex increased slightly. For FY 2026 it was £1.34 billion compared to £1.24 billion in FY 2025.

Even so, Tesco generated an unadjusted FCF of just over £2.5 billion.

That was more than enough for dividends (£937 million) and share buybacks (£1.54 billion) for the whole year.

I'll talk more about FCF later, but for now you can see that they're still a sort of large-scale cash cow.

Now 'The Bad'.

Tesco is leaning into price to keep up with Aldi, but there's a trade-off: margins get squeezed as spreads tighten. So even with revenue growing around ~5.4%, the bottom line isn't keeping the same pace.

But first, I'll show you the gross margin:

Here you can see that the gross margin probably peaked at ~7.6%, but it's still above the 5-year average at 6.8%.

And what about 'The Ugly'?

For me, the ugly duckling of the thesis is still the Booker Group.

And don't get me wrong. It was to be expected since Booker is more affected by slow foodservice traffic.

For example, in the entire FY 2026, Booker saw same-store sales 0.2% higher year-over-year. Much lower than Tesco's growth, don't you think?

For EBIT, Booker grew a little more. About 0.7% to £292 million.

But you know what? There's still beauty here.

Booker Group is the cyclical lever inside Tesco.

When foodservice is strong, it helps offset the slower growth in the core grocery business. They also have a B2B logistics service, which has fat margins and is practically asset-light compared to Tesco.

That's why I put them in 'The Ugly' category.

Ugly can become beautiful someday.

My Projections for FY 2027

Management shared a bit of color on FY 2027.

Not much, but it's a good start.

For example, for EBIT, they are targeting something between £3 and £3.3 billion. If I take the revenue projection for Wall St. for Tesco ($101.37 billion or £74.98 billion already converted), this is an EBIT margin of 4 to 4.4%.

So, since the midpoint EBIT margin is at 4.2%, it seems to me that it will remain flat again.

They also targeted £1.6 billion in Capex and at least £750 million in additional buybacks.

I'm also going to pinpoint a flat EBITDA margin of ~6% (£4.5 billion, growing slightly less than 3% year-over-year).

I also think an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate of ~80% here.

It has been quite volatile in recent years due to variations in operating assets.

This would be ~£3.6 billion, and with this guided Capex, the unadjusted FCF would be £2 billion.

My projection falls within the medium-term range they guided (£1.5 to £2 billion).

Also modeling a mid-single-digit buyback yield (around 5%), the outstanding shares would be around 6 billion.

Is It Still a Buy Opportunity?

I'll keep an eye on the multiples here.

I think companies like Loblaw, Metro, Ahold Delhaize (ADRNY), Marks and Spencer (MAKSY), and J Sainsbury (JSAIY) are interesting peers.

Starting with EV/EBITDA, the average multiple of these companies is ~9x. Plugging in £4.5 billion for the entire FY 2026 and dividing by ~2 billion shares outstanding for the ADR (as I imagine they'll reduce the outstanding shares by 5% by the end of the year, this gives something close to 2 billion shares outstanding in the ADR), I find between $19.50 and $20.00.

Notice that, looking at it this way, the upside is almost zero.

The average P/E ($1.29) also gives a tiny upside up to $23.10 per share.

But are we looking at the wrong timeframe?

For slow-and-steady names like mature grocers, I usually lean on the Graham's Model. With the ADR trading around ~$19.90, you're looking at roughly a ~4% implied growth rate off a $1.29 Year 1 EPS before applying the ~25% haircut I typically build in.

Wall St. is penciling in 8.3% CAGR through FY 2030, which gets me to a price target of around $22 after applying my usual 25% margin of safety.

To me, there's a path to the mid-$20s if they can put up double-digit EPS growth over the next few years.

It’s possible, but I don’t think the market is willing to pay up for that just yet.

To finish all this off, I can model a Gordon Model here.

Using the $0.58 annual payout and assuming long-term growth in line with the historical ~4.3% CAGR since 1992, plus a ~6.65% cost of equity (4.6% risk-free from the U.K. 10-year, ~0.6 beta, and ~8% market return), I land at a price target of around $24.

Here's a peek at what I found in each model and the average:

At ~$19.90, you're looking at roughly ~12% upside to my $22.30 target. Add a mid-single-digit shareholder yield, and you’re just shy of a ~20% total return from here.

So this isn't really a post-Christmas re-rating story.

I'm going to 'Hold' Tesco at these prices.

It's a strong compounder, but it's not a bargain now like it was when we got the timing right before.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Allbirds: Laying Tracks Ahead Of The Train
data.description:
I bought shoes from Allbirds (BIRD) once. It was because my boss wanted me to wear something other than sandals to the office. I returned them after I stopped working there. And now I hear about them again because they're going to do AI. Well, time to get a pulse on this.

Shoes Weren't Working

I guess the intriguing thing is that 2022 is when I briefly bought a pair. Revenue also peaked that year, and it should tell you something. They didn't have a profitable year. In a couple years, they were in their "strategic transformation" era.

I looked up their earnings call from about a year ago. One thing in particular stood out to me. Listen to CEO Joe Vernachio talk about branding:

We developed a four-part long-form series intended to create narratives that pull in our customers and make them feel connected to our brand in a way that lasts.

The brand was weak. Strong brands don't have management that says, "Make them feel connected. Brand is cost, comfort, and style. It's hard to establish with a marketing campaign. You can't make people feel things. It's not a surprise they threw in the towel on this. It's probably for the best."

Pivot to AI

Even in Q3 last year, the transformation plan for shoes was still a go. But Allbirds shrank again. They got their 10K out and showed this. Suddenly they cancelled their earnings call. In the same announcement, they said they're selling the shoe business. The final piece came out two weeks later: AI. So they are going to acquire server assets and rent them out. After that, they'll be NewBird AI.

It's easy to understand why they shifted to this. Demand is huge, which wasn't the case for their old business. Compute is compute, not much of a brand problem. They just need assets that people will pay to use. Hyperscalers are trying to get every GPU they can. Is there logic in assuming this trickles down to smaller players? Probably. The prospective NewBird is a bet on that idea.

Bizarre Outlook

BIRD is one of the strangest stocks I've ever looked at. Their announcement was like a pharma company that had a breakthrough.

The market cap grew a lot. It went from $22M to $94M. BIRD's not huge from that, but it could get more from ATM offerings down the road. Rarely do I see companies not take advantage of it. I think dilution is likely, but how much is unclear. For now, they have the $50M facility.

So that's one thing. Then it depends on what they buy with it. Hyperscalers probably eat up the Blackwell supply for the foreseeable future. Small players like NewBird can buy older products secondhand. Pricing suggests that the facility could snag them 1K-2K in GPUs for a first data center. So it depends on unit prices. It also depends on rental rates, but those are strong lately. Even a second-rate site can make money.

Another thing is execution. First, the shareholders have to approve the $50M credit facility. Then management has to make moves with it. We don't even know if it will be the same management. At least some personnel rotation would be normal here.

And that execution affects when NewBird would make money. They have to build these assets, and that can take a couple of years. Their best hope would be contracts that offer some kind of prepayment by the customer to mitigate their cash burn in the meantime. Who knows if that happens?

The Ups and Downs

Maybe predecessors can illustrate the upside if execution is good. CoreWeave (CRWV) is a good example of how this execution looks.

Numbers show that it took a couple years to leverage $3.9B of assets into $5.1B of revenue while still building that balance sheet. That's why they went public: to raise capital. It's a complex process of laying down tracks while the train moves. It's conceivable that NewBird can do the same at a smaller scale. Perhaps their market cap grows if this nears profitability.

But NewBird will have virtually no assets to start. That means a lot of track to lay first. The upside of execution can be easily diluted away when they raise money for that. And they might not execute well.

This is all thinking long-term. Near-term, there is also the special dividend. The sale of their shoe business is a $39M deal. The announcement said the dividend would follow for shareholders of record as of May 20th. It's implied that the proceeds are the dividend. What does this mean in real terms?

Well, they have 8.7M common shares right now. $39M would pay $4.48 per share. Costs of the transaction would whittle it down. So maybe not all get paid. If you had BIRD at $2.50, you get that cost basis back, probably some profit. At the current price, $10.80, I think it could be a return of 30% to 40%.

There's $26.7M in cash and $17.3M of debt on the 10K. Working capital shifts could affect the dividend by a few million, plus overhead costs. Plus, management may want to keep some for the pivot.

That's counting Class B shares. They will get this dividend with Class. That's their incentive. They get some money back after their shoe business didn't pan out.

Prefer to Wait and See

I inferred a lot from a few paragraphs in one press release. That's all they've really said. It's possible that management hasn't fully decided their course yet. It's likely a better path than the old one. There's a big special dividend and high demand for AI. The costs, financing, and dilution are mysteries, though.

There's no reason for investors to bet for or against this one right now. See if the market cap falls to $39M. At least then the special dividend covers the cost.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PCMM: Turning Bearish On This Private Credit CLO Fund
data.description:
Thesis

We first started covering the BondBloxx Private Credit CLO ETF (PCMM) a year ago, shortly after the name came to market. We find CLO ETFs to be a great innovation, and we articulated that in the first coverage ever on Seeking Alpha of the mammoth Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA). We were the first to cover that name and structure, and articulated in our PCMM article how asset mangers are now able to put into the ETF wrapper any niche segments from the CLO market.

PCMM focuses on CLOs backed by loans to middle market companies, a segment which had never been accessible to retail investors before. In today's article we are going to highlight why we are bearish this private credit CLO exchange traded fund in today's macro.

The fund basics

The fund has grown since our last coverage:

Just like other segments of private credit, the fund has seen a significant AUM growth in 2025, with assets reaching over $180 M late last year.

While other CLO ETFs offer exposure to CLOs containing widely syndicated loans, PCMM focuses on CLOs containing middle-market loans:

PCMM invests in at least 80% of its net assets in private credit collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) in which the majority of each such collateralized loan obligation consists of a pool of loans to private companies. The ETF provides targeted exposure to loans to middle market companies - key drivers of U.S. economic growth - that have traditionally only been accessible to institutional and high net worth investors.

Middle market companies are smaller enterprises which do not always get the same focus from banks as large corporates:

The graph above gives investors an idea of how the business community defines middle market when enterprise value is considered. While large companies get substantial coverage from banks when it comes to capital markets needs, middle market enterprises are sometimes forgotten or mis-priced when it comes to debt needs. A large company can provide a number of revenue opportunities through various business segments, thus attracting substantial sales coverage from the banking community.

Loans made to middle market companies are not as liquid as the widely syndicated ones, and sometimes they come with tailored financial solutions. PCMM as an ETF takes CLOs backed by such loans and puts them in an exchange traded fund.

PCMM holdings

The ETF is overweight private credit CLO tranches:

  • Private Credit CLO: 88%
  • Broadly Syndicated Loan CLO: 10%
  • Cash: residual

From a ratings perspective the ETF represents a vertical slice of the capital structure:

The fund is overweight AAA tranches and BBB tranches, which represent 35% and 24% respectively of the holdings. Please note that BB tranches are only 14% of the fund - this aspect is very important because AAA to BB tranches are always floating rate, while some BB tranches can be fixed rate. This makes PCMM a largely floating rate fund, which means that lower Fed Funds will result in lower net cash flows.

Some fund analytics are:

  • Nr. of securities: 81
  • Duration: 0.08 years
  • OAS: 309 bps
  • SEC yield: 6.15%

Lower Fed Funds will equal lower yields

Even though the recent Iran war has shifted the yield curve up and lowered expectations for Fed cuts, President Trump has been very vocal about wanting lower Fed Funds:

Earlier this week, Trump repeated his assertion that interest rates should be much lower than the current range of 3.5% to 3.75%. Asked during an interview on Fox Business Network if he thought rates would go lower later this year, Trump replied: "When Kevin gets in, I do."

If the Iran war is solved quickly, the market will shift back expectations to what we saw in the beginning of the year, which is 1 or 2 Fed cuts in 2026. The political will is clearly tilted towards more cuts, but it is unclear how the new Fed chair will be able to implement that view.

Lower Fed Funds means lower SOFR rates, which in turn means lower net yields on PCMM. The current SEC yield of 6.15% is therefore set to move lower, with one for one translation from Fed cuts (i.e. if the Fed cuts by 50 bps, expect the SEC yield on the fund to decrease by that amount).

We have seen in the past year significant outflows from floating rate leveraged loans into fixed rate bonds, exactly because of this macro dynamic. Expect the flows to resume once Warsh is installed as Fed Chair.

Spreads are too low

What we have seen in the BDC space is a massive outflow of funds and decrease in prices once defaults have picked up in the BDC portfolios:

People concerned with defaults increased the required spreads on the underlying portfolios thus causing the above price moves. In the middle market space the spreads are a bit more visible than pure direct lending, but they ultimately represent a beta play of widely syndicated spreads:

The above charts, courtesy of Palmer Square, represent the secondary spreads for various widely syndicated CLOs by rating. As we can see the common theme is much tighter spreads: AAA tranches for example trade at 100 bps over SOFR, the tightest level since 2011! Middle market CLO tranches trade with a slightly higher spread (anywhere from 20 bps to 80 bps for highly rated paper), but have a very high correlation to the widely syndicated market.

As an investor you want to buy a fund like PCMM when spreads are wide (like in 2022), and sell PCMM when spreads are this tight. We can see the massive widening in spreads across the board in 2022, when AAA tranches came close to a 250 bps discount margin. While PCMM was not around in 2022, we can look at proxies such as other ETFs that focus on AAA and BBB names, respectively:

Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) focuses on AAA names, while Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB) focuses on BBB names. We can see the two names with -4% and -14% drawdowns, respectively, in 2022 when spreads moved wider. Since PCMM contains middle market CLOs, expect larger drawdowns for those.

The current macro is poor for PCMM: on one hand we have a Fed which will resume cuts soon thus decreasing net yields, while on the other hand secondary spreads are historically low, creating the potential for deep drawdowns if we do get a recession. We see much more downside than upside here (virtually no upside outside the dividend yield given current spreads).

Conclusion

PCMM is a fixed income ETF. The name focuses on middle market CLO tranches and represents a vertical slice of the CLO capital structure. The name comes from BondBloxx, an asset manager which we respect and appreciate for what it does in the fixed income space. The current macro is very unfavorable for PCMM with Fed cuts on the horizon again and secondary spreads for CLOs historically tight. We are a 'Sell' for PCMM in the current macro environment.

data.title: Turkcell: Cheap Valuation, But Rising CapEx And Forex Risks (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Five years ago, I first wrote about Turkcell Iletisim Hizmetleri A.S. (TKC), rating it a buy on the grounds that it was cheap given the amount of growth it was offering. Since then the stock has performed respectably, despite a challenging environment, giving investors somewhat better than market average returns.

Dividends included, this was a solid run for Turkcell, but by 2026 it looks like a very different company, with a larger number of subscribers (46.2 million as of 12/31/25) and necessarily a slower percentage of growth than what we were seeing five years ago. At the same time, it has been showing up on a lot of screens lately as potentially undervalued relative to other foreign telecoms, so I think it’s worthwhile to reexamine the company and see whether this is another opportunity or if there are reasons for a discount to the sector.

Top Line Strength and Underlying Value

Last month, Turkcell came in with its FY2025 results, capping off the year with yet more top line growth and a bottom line in which earnings for continuing operations grew as well.

FY2024

FY2025

Total Revenue

$4.87 billion

$5.39 billion

Gross Profit

$1.53 billion

Operating Income

$1.03 billion

$1.64 billion

($1.6 billion)

($2.0 billion)

Net Income

$687 million

$393 million

EPADS (Continuing Ops)

37¢

46¢

EPADS (Total)

79¢

45¢

(Source: Turkcell Press Release)

The first thing you’ll notice here, which supports why Turkcell is showing up on screeners as potentially undervalued, is that while they’re no longer doubling their revenue every five years like they used to, there’s still meaningful growth on the top line, and more importantly, improvements in both growth and operating margins.

That gives us a Turkcell with growing continuing operations that are increasingly profitable, which is an excellent sign. At the current prices, the P/E ratio is 14.71, which is somewhat lower than the sector median of 19.46. Cash flow was also extremely strong, with Turkcell continuing to pay a respectable dividend roughly in line with the sector and announcing the beginning of a three year stock buyback plan.

Forward earnings trends look positive too. The consensus estimate for FY2026 is $6.79 billion and $0.70 EPADS. That's a forward P/E of 9.46, which again is quite a bit better than the sector median of 16.07.

Given their cash flow, the balance sheet looks rather stable as well.

Cash and Equivalents

$2.05 billion

Long-Term Debt

$2.74

Shareholder Equity

$5.79 billion

(Source: Turkcell Press Release)

This amounts to a price/book ratio of almost exactly 1.00, which again is better than the sector's 1.80. For a company generating this kind of cash flow to be trading so relatively cheaply to underlying book as well as to future anticipated earnings is really unusual without a very good reason. Barring serious risk factors, it would be fair to believe Turkcell is being undervalued by the market.

At the same time, there’s an obvious question here that’s screaming to be addressed.

Soaring CapEx: Turkcell Bets on Data Centers

The mobile network is only one aspect of Turkcell’s business, as they try to spread into Fintech, grow their broadband offerings, and, perhaps most importantly, invest in data centers. Turkcell has designs on turning Turkey into a hyperscale cloud region, and their partnership with Google is a step toward that, one that’s costing both companies a lot of money to build out.

The challenge here, and the underlying risk, is that while Google is perennially flush with cash and can afford to throw billions at projects like this, Turkcell is a much smaller company. They clearly have strong cash flow and can afford growing CapEx, to a point, but this heavy investment is a gamble that needs to pay off, because Turkcell isn’t so big they can afford to shrug off such a sizable investment in data centers.

The most recent conference call pointed to the logic behind it—while overall business revenue was only up 11% year over year, Turkcell saw their data and cloud business grow by 32%. There’s the beginning of meaningful demand here, seemingly, and importantly, it would offer a chance for Turkcell to return to the high-end growth they were offering shareholders a few years ago, before the mobile business had fully matured.

Data centers and cloud computing are the hot ticket the world over, so it’s no surprise that Turkcell, too, is trying to get in on it. The costs are substantial, however, and if the demand for cloud service growth dries up, they could find themselves with a lot of capacity and insufficient customers to fill it. That is a serious risk, as I see it, because again, they’re just one of many companies dumping money into data centers, and there will likely be winners and losers when the dust settles. Whether Turkcell proves to be the former or the latter remains to be seen.

There is another risk that’s not obvious from the operation statements, though, and for that, we have to dig deeper into the conference call for a comment that aged very poorly.

The Forex Risk: Turkcell Seeks to Avoid Hedging

With their operations centering overwhelmingly in Turkey, it makes sense that Turkcell does the overwhelming majority of their business in Turkish lira. That creates a foreign exchange exposure, as Turkcell does business in other currencies with a lot of companies. That has historically meant holding substantial derivatives for the sake of hedging to avoid foreign exchange losses.

Here’s where the problem comes in. CFO Kamil Kalyon saw the Turkish Central Bank’s policy as working very successfully and argued in the conference call that instead of continuing with increasingly expensive hedging, the company “prefer to move a short position in the U.S. FX side in 2025.”

That would work well if the Turkish lira remains stable, Kalyon notes, saying the strategy worked in 2025, and “if you do not have any war in Iran or something like that, we believe that in 2026 this policy also will work.” That’s obviously an immediate problem, because we very much did have a war in Iran already in 2026, and the Turkish lira has not handled it particularly well.

While the lira hasn’t completely fallen off a cliff, it’s clear that the exchange rate is continuing to trend downward, and while it's fair to think the Turkish Central Bank has gotten inflation somewhat under control compared to the staggering levels of 2022, it’s still floating around 30%, which does not lend itself to a lira-heavy business unwinding its forex hedging yet.

So far, the war has not led to any disastrous drops in the lira that would immediately portend a huge crisis for Turkcell, but the risk nevertheless remains, and the example of a war in Iran, which is still unresolved, is just one of any number of geopolitical events that could weaken the lira and therefore impact Turkcell’s bottom line. This is an added risk that, in my opinion, Turkcell simply didn’t need right now.

A Good Company but a Bad Time

So where do we land on Turkcell? While I see the case for the company to be relatively cheap compared to the sector and therefore potentially appealing, I can’t ignore the obvious risks of foreign exchange loss and uncertainty surrounding the still growing CapEx chasing the data center business. Turkcell may be discounted, but I think it’s fairly so in these circumstances, so I’m rating them a hold at present.

Investors should keep an eye on whether or not the heavy investment in data centers is leading not only to immediate growth but also to growth that is commensurate with the expenses they’re realizing. And don’t forget to keep an eye on the Turkish lira because that’s another risk here.

Turkcell absolutely has the potential to get itself back to a level of growth that would allow them to continue to provide better-than-average returns to investors, but I see the mounting costs chasing those returns as a potentially risky path, and the company is something better watched from a safe distance for the time being.

data.title: Nike: Please Just Don't Do It
data.description:
One of the biggest shifts in my investing philosophy over the years occurred when I began to realize the importance of truly taking into consideration the quality of the businesses over the long term. For example, in the short term, share price is moved by market sentiment, leading to changes in a stock's valuation multiple. But over the long term, it's earnings growth that pushes the share price higher. Of course, valuation still plays an incredibly important role, but even if you buy a stock at a valuation multiple that is a bit above the average valuation multiple for the S&P 500, you are likely to do quite well if the company is growing earnings at a much faster rate than the S&P 500 as a whole.

So naturally, the question of determining the quality of a business generally can be narrowed down to two key metrics:

  1. Return on invested capital
  2. Changes in profitability ratio

A high return on invested capital (assuming the company has a high reinvestment rate) will lead to high earnings growth. An increasing gross profit margin typically indicates a company has a competitive advantage in the form of pricing power, which, of course, also leads to faster earnings growth. This is the primary reason high-quality stocks tend to trade at a premium valuation and still deliver above-average returns.

But what rarely gets talked about is the double-edged sword that this creates when the quality of the company starts to deteriorate. When the quality of a company drops, companies face a double-edged sword in the sense that their future returns suffer due to:

  1. A drop in earnings growth rates, and as a result...
  2. A drop in the valuation multiple

And this is exactly what has happened to Nike stock.

NIKE (NKE) has declined roughly 28% year to date and touched a new 52-week and 10-year low of $42.09 during the week of April 13, 2026, before bouncing to its current price of around $46. Over a five-year horizon, NKE has lost approximately 62.5% of its value (including dividends), while the S&P 500 has returned roughly 71%, which is a staggering 130 percentage point divergence for a company that was considered a quality blue-chip compounder for most of its history. At this point, it would be natural for value-oriented investors to ask whether the selloff has created an entry point.

When we consider that question through the lens of a 'risk to reward' perspective, despite the company being half the market cap it was just a few years ago, I still believe the answer is still a resounding no. Let's review what went wrong and why I fear things could potentially get even worse.

The Dividend

Nike currently has its highest starting yield in the last decade. The dividend growth has been attractive, but of course this is primarily due to the drastic drop in share price. And with the company's drop in free cash flow while simultaneously growing their dividend payments, the free cash flow payout ratio has climbed to slightly above 70%.

In fact, free cash flow is now at the lowest levels since 2020, sitting at $3.26 billion. And with what management has indicated to us, we can expect another poor year in terms of free cash flow generation. Free cash flow (specifically on a per-share basis) is the ultimate driver of intrinsic value over the long term, so a decline in free cash flow has ultimately led to the decrease in share price. So what contributed to this decline in free cash flow?

The Issues

In my opinion, Nike's issues over the last few years can be boiled down to three fundamental issues:

  1. Revenue declining
  2. Gross margins declining
  3. Generating lower returns on invested capital

Each of these declining metrics indicates various issues with the company. Declining revenue suggests weakening demand and a loss of market share, particularly as competitors like Adidas and Lululemon continue to execute at a high level and capture consumer attention. At the same time, shrinking gross margins point to reduced pricing power and increased promotional activity, which is often a sign that a brand is losing its premium positioning. And perhaps most concerning of them all is the decline in return on invested capital, which signals that Nike is no longer allocating capital as efficiently as it once did, meaning each dollar reinvested into the business is generating less incremental profit than in prior years. Of course, each of these plays a major role in the overall free cash flow generated by Nike.

With all of these accounted for, we have a bit of background as to why we have seen such a drastic sell-off in the last five years. Has this halving in market cap created an opportunity? Let's review what management has indicated moving forward.

Turn Around?

The Q3 FY2026 earnings report (released in late March 2026) offered very little comfort for those looking for the turnaround play to gain momentum. Revenue was essentially flat on a year-over-year basis, with footwear up a modest 2%, apparel flat at $3.2B, equipment down 2%, and Converse down an astounding 30%! On top of that, margins continued to decline, with gross margin contracting a further 130 basis points to 40.2%, so now sitting materially below both the five- and ten-year averages, as we saw above. For Q4 FY2026, management guided for revenue to decline 2–4% with gross margin down an additional 25–75 basis points. So not only do the issues that put them in this position persist, but they are even getting worse. But the pain doesn't just end there...

China continues to be a major issue for Nike. China sales are expected to decline approximately 20% in Q4 FY2026. Keep in mind, this was a region that was once expected to be a long-term secular growth engine for the brand. On top of the issues in China, tariff dynamics are adding further to the issues for Nike. Management initially estimated a $1.0B annual tariff headwind but, as of the Q1 FY2026 earnings call, raised that figure to $1.5B after new reciprocal tariff rates took effect, which added a 50% increase in a single quarter.

CFO Matthew Friend noted-

The company expects a 120-basis-point gross margin headwind from tariffs in FY2026, up from the prior estimate of 75 basis points.

Nike has previously stated that they are working to reduce China-sourced footwear imports from 16% to the high-single-digit range by the end of fiscal year 2026, but this sourcing shift is a multi-quarter process with uncertain timing, so I wouldn't count on this making much of a difference from a financial standpoint in the short term.

So in short, all of the issues that put them in this position are still persisting and even worsening, and they are now also dealing with more issues in China as well as a tariff issue that will cost them around $1.5 billion annually, which, for reference, would be almost half of their free cash flow from 2025. The business is fundamentally weaker.

However, of course, this is a turnaround play we are talking about. Has the sell-off gone too far, leading to what Warren Buffett would describe as a 'cigar butt' investing opportunity?

Valuation

There is currently a major issue in terms of trying to value Nike stock appropriately right now.

Management has explicitly stated it will not provide full-year or long-term guidance and intends to wait until its fall Investor Day to address the outlook.

This decision places investors interested in Nike in an exceptionally difficult position. Without visibility into full-year revenue, margin targets, or any framework for FCF recovery, any discounted cash flow model is built on assumptions that cannot be anchored to any reality. Hypothetically speaking, if you can tell me confidently what the future cash flows of the business will be, I can accurately give you a reasonable valuation range for the stock. As of right now, we cannot project what future cash flows will look like for Nike, which further pushes the stock to be valued at a lower valuation multiple.

However, what we can do is invert the process by using a reverse discounted cash flow analysis model to see how much free cash flow growth the market is pricing in.

To my dismay, the market is still pricing in a 6.8% free cash flow CAGR over the next decade for Nike. This is despite the fact that we've seen it is very likely the next few years we see little to no, or even negative, free cash flow growth in the coming years. The market is pricing this in, despite the fact management has not issued any guidance for the next year. This is quite concerning.

To add on to this, despite the massive drop in share price, Nike is still trading very close to its historical valuation multiples. This is, of course, due to the fact they have seen a drop in earnings along with the share price decline.

Both their forward and TTM P/E multiples are even well above many of the Magnificent 7 stocks. Even after an over 50% drop in share price, when looked at through the lens of a reverse DCF model and a multiple valuation, it becomes clear there is still more potential downside for Nike considering the crumbling fundamentals.

The Chance of a Turnaround

To be fair, Nike is still one of the most recognizable brands on the planet, and that type of brand equity does not disappear overnight. For me to turn bullish on Nike, I would need to see a very specific set of developments. First, gross margins would need to begin trending back toward at least the 43-44% range, which would indicate that the company is regaining pricing power and pulling back on the heavy promotional activity that has eroded profitability. Second, I would need to see revenue growth return, even if modestly, as a sign that market share losses to competitors like Adidas and Lululemon are being contained. Third, and perhaps most importantly, management needs to provide clear, long-term guidance at the upcoming fall Investor Day. Without a concrete roadmap for how they plan to return to a 10%+ operating margin and grow free cash flow at a rate that supports the current valuation as well as the dividend, there is simply no way to underwrite this stock with any level of confidence.

It is also worth noting that the 2026 FIFA World Cup, hosted in North America this summer, could provide a near-term revenue catalyst for Nike's footwear and apparel segments. And if trade tensions with China ease, the roughly 20% decline in Greater China sales could reverse faster than expected. These are the primary upside scenarios that could help provide more of an immediate recovery from a fundamental standpoint. However, while I do consider these as short-term catalysts, it would likely take at least a year for this to be realized in the underlying fundamentals.

Final Thoughts

Nike was once the textbook example of a high-quality compounder; however, it has fallen victim to the double-edged sword of declining earnings and valuation multiple. Declining revenue, shrinking margins, and weakening returns on invested capital have become more than just temporary headwinds. When you layer in continued pressure from China, rising tariff costs, and a lack of clear forward guidance, it becomes increasingly difficult to justify the market’s expectations for long-term free cash flow growth. Even after a significant drawdown, the stock does not appear to offer a compelling margin of safety, especially from a risk-to-reward perspective. Until we see tangible improvement in the underlying business, investing in Nike still poses far too many risks and a high level of unpredictability.

data.title: lululemon Won't Remain Cheap For Too Long
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about lululemon (LULU) in January, I called it my "contrarian retail pick for 2026."

Three months into the post, the stock has not yet delivered positive returns. On the contrary, the stock is down close to 16% since the time of my publication, underperforming the S&P 500 during this period of time.

This is because the timing of the inflection is taking longer than I originally expected, while analysts have revised their forward growth estimates lower while still expecting revenue and earnings growth to inflect higher from current levels.

The way I see it, the bar is set quite low at this point for lululemon to beat and raise, especially as its Americas business shows early signs of inflection when excluding the 53rd week from 2024, while management doubles down on its full-price strategy to protect its premium positioning as well as its margins.

As a result, I continue to remain optimistic about the company and its stock and reiterate my “buy” rating, especially as its forward PE ratio has further contracted to 13.5x since my previous post.

Americas Shows Early Signs of a Turnaround, While International Momentum Continues

lululemon reported its Q4 FY25 earnings last month, where the company beat expectations on both revenue and earnings per share.

On the revenue front, lululemon’s revenue slowed on a sequential basis, growing 1% YoY to $3.64B, with global comparable sales growing 3%.

Out of the $3.6B in revenue, Americas revenue decreased at a faster rate compared to the previous quarter, at 4%, to $2.7B, contributing 74% of total revenue, down from 77% last year. However, excluding the 53rd week of 2024, Americas net revenue was flat, which should be considered an improvement from the previous quarter, where it declined 2%.

During the earnings call, management discussed their strategic focus on returning their North America business to healthier levels of full-price sales, driven by product newness, SKU reduction, and operating discipline, particularly through rebalancing of inventory levels. At the same time, management also discussed their strategic priorities on enhancing their guest experience, both in-store and online, through better localization, a superior design playbook, and activity-based merchandising that allows for better storytelling and improves the shopping experience for guests.

Turning our attention to the International business, both China and Rest of World continued to grow at a much faster rate than the Americas market, with contributions to total revenue growing on a year-over-year basis.

When it comes to Mainland China, revenues grew 24% YoY to $528.4M, contributing 15% of total revenue, up from 12% last year. Similar to the Americas market, if we exclude the 53rd week from 2024, the growth rate is considerably better at 32% on a year-over-year basis, though slower on a sequential basis. Meanwhile, the Rest of World also grew their revenues by 10%, or 16% when excluding the 53rd week, contributing 12% of total revenue, up from 11% last year, as guests continue to resonate strongly with their product assortment as well as their localized approach to marketing.

A Bottom is Approaching for Profit Margins

When it comes to profitability, both gross and operating margins declined on a year-over-year basis. When it comes to gross profits, margins declined by 550 basis points to 54.9%, driven by higher tariffs and increased markdowns, while operating margins also contracted by 660 basis points to 22.3% as SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue expanded from their ongoing investments in brand awareness.

Moving forward, I remain optimistic about margins improving from current levels, especially as management is focused on returning their North America business to full-price sales, along with increasing efficiencies across inventory management and supply chain, while increasingly leveraging automation and AI opportunities to unlock operating margin.

Plus, when we look at forward earnings per share growth projections in the coming quarters, we can see that Q1 is set to mark a bottom, which should be followed by an improving trend higher, as can be seen below. Note that for the full year FY26, management has guided for gross and operating margins to decrease 120 and 250 basis points, respectively, but we should see a bottoming in Q1 and Q2 before we see an inflection higher.

lululemon Has a Low Bar to Clear as Growth Expectations Are Revised Lower

So, while at a fundamental level, lululemon’s business looks set to ascend higher for both revenue and earnings per share based on consensus estimates, the forward trend in analysts' revisions is trending lower, as can be seen below.

Plus, the company is yet to appoint an official CEO, which brings a degree of uncertainty as well, as Meghan Frank and Andre Maestrini serve as interim co-CEOs while carrying on their respective roles as CFO and Chief Commercial Officer, respectively.

The way I see it, lululemon has a very low bar to deliver a surprise at this point. In other words, if lululemon can beat and raise, there is a strong probability that the stock will be rerated higher from current levels. Based on the early inflection point for its Americas business with the strategic shift to full-price sales, there is a strong chance for the company to deliver better than expected results, given that the segment is the largest revenue contributor.

Meanwhile, the stock is trading at a forward PE ratio of 13.58x, which is 56% lower than its 5-year average PE ratio. Note, this is significantly cheaper compared to NIKE (NKE) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK), which are trading at 30.81 and 16.35x, respectively.

Assuming that lululemon can indeed demonstrate a bottoming in its earnings in Q1, followed by an inflection higher in the mid- to high-single-digit range, I believe it should be trading at par with the 10-year average S&P 500 PE ratio of 18.8x, which, when applied to its FY29 earnings per share estimates of $14.39, translates to a price target of $270 per share. This represents an upside potential of 61% from current levels, while the downside risk to Wall Street’s lowest price target is roughly 10%, making the risk-reward quite attractive at current levels.

Risks and Conclusions

The risk-reward for lululemon looks attractive indeed, as we should see a fundamental inflection in revenue and earnings growth from Q2 onwards.

Note that the inflection needs to be supported by the Americas' market, where the management is doubling down on its “full price” strategy.

However, in the short term, the Iran war may have complicated the sales growth outlook in the Americas market as inflation expectations have risen.

From a technical standpoint, the stock also needs to clear its 200DMA at $185.19/share, which I believe will take place from a fundamental re-rating higher.

The way I see it, lululemon is not out of the woods yet. It still has to demonstrate an inflection in both its revenue and earnings growth, which, if it fails, could lead to some more short-term pain.

However, on the other hand, if the company can successfully stage a meaningful acceleration in its Americas market through its full-price strategy, I believe this would be an exciting investment opportunity at an attractive risk-reward, which is why I will continue to remain invested and rate the stock a “buy” at current levels.

data.title: India Gold Market Update: Mixed Reading
data.description:
Highlights

  • Gold prices partially recovered in April1 following the sharp March decline, while import curbs and supply bottlenecks narrowed domestic discounts
  • Listed jewellers posted strong y/y growth in Q1 2026, driven by wedding as well as discretionary spends, higher ticket sizes, and ongoing expansion
  • Redemptions dampened gold ETF inflows, but holdings and investor participation continued to rise
  • Digital gold purchases remained firm, well above recent averages
  • Reserve Bank of India’s gold reserve holdings remained steady amid a decline in total forex reserves
  • Imports hit 9-month low in March.

Looking ahead

  • Demand is expected to be supported by the summer wedding season and key buying occasions such as Akshaya Tritiya2 and regional festivals, with price stability driving buying interest.

Partial price recovery following sharp March decline

Gold prices declined sharply in March in both US dollar and INR terms, marking the weakest monthly performance in nearly 13 and 12 years, respectively. Prices fell by 12% in USD and 8% in INR terms, with the relatively lower decline in domestic prices linked to the depreciation in the INR (by 4%). The price decline was driven by momentum factors – outflows from global gold ETFs and an unwind of COMEX net long positions, alongside higher opportunity costs due to a stronger US dollar and higher bond yields.

As of 13 April, prices had partially recovered and stabilised, although remained more than 13% below the January peak (Chart 1).3 The rebound has been supported by the persistent geopolitical risk premium, a softer US dollar and a pickup in inflows into global ETFs. On a y-t-d basis, gold prices remain elevated, up 8% at US$4,722/oz and 13% at INR 149,494/10g.4

Domestic gold prices, which had been trading at a sustained discount to international prices since mid-February, saw discounts widen further in March – from an average of US$15/oz to US$46/oz. Discounts have since narrowed significantly, averaging ~US$8/oz over the first two weeks of April.

The narrowing is attributed to tighter supply conditions following curbs on imports of platinum alloys (containing more than 1% gold by weight), along with broader restrictions on gold, silver, and platinum jewellery imports.5 In addition, anecdotal reports suggest operational bottlenecks, particularly delays in customs clearance of bullion consignments of nominated banks, further tightened supply.

Soft jewellery demand

Demand remained muted in the first half of March, impacted by seasonal and financial year-end factors as well as price volatility. Market feedback suggests that purchases were deferred during the price spike. However, as prices eased around regional festivals, demand improved but was overall subdued. Retailers focused on marketing campaigns and promotional offers to drive sales. Exchange of old gold jewellery remained a key contributor, reportedly accounting for 40–50% of sales, while investment demand for bars and coins continued to be robust.

Strong Q1 2026 for listed jewellers

Listed jewellery retailers6 delivered a strong performance in Q1 2026 (Jan–Mar), with revenue growth ranging from 32% to 124% y/y, making it the best quarter of the financial year for several retailers. Growth was driven by a robust wedding season and steady discretionary demand, which remained resilient despite the elevated and volatile gold prices. Regional festivals also supported sales. Retailers also benefited from an increase in the average ticket size of purchases, growth in plain gold jewellery, and a sharp rise in coin sales. Additionally, digital channels continued to gain traction, with some jewellers reporting over a fourfold y/y growth in online revenues.

Retailers continued their expansion push, adding between 7 to 38 stores during the quarter, although some launches were delayed due to geopolitical supply disruptions. Most retailers have indicated plans to continue expanding their store network, reflecting confidence in long-term demand and a focus to deepen market presence.

Overall, the quarter highlighted strong domestic demand resilience alongside strategic growth initiatives.

Gold ETFs: redemptions weigh on net inflows

Despite significant redemptions and in contrast with the trend of North American and European ETFs, Indian gold ETFs extended their inflow streak to the 11th consecutive month in March 2026, recording net inflows of INR 22.7bn (US$244mn) as per data from the Association of Mutual Funds of India (AMFI), above our initial estimate.7 This, however, was the lowest monthly net inflow in seven months (Chart 3). The moderation was primarily driven by record redemptions of INR 31.6bn (US$341mn), likely reflecting continued profit-taking, a trend carried forward from February. Nevertheless, cumulative gold holdings rose to 115t as of end March, in line with our initial estimates, indicating underlying resilient demand.

Following the relative weakness in March, April has seen a recovery in flows. During 1–10 April, net inflows stood at INR 17.36bn (US$187mn), contributing an additional 1.2t to collective holdings.

Q1 2026 recorded the strongest quarterly inflows into Indian gold ETFs, with net inflows of INR 316bn (US$3.45bn), resulting in an addition of approximately 20t to total holdings. Notably, nearly 80% of these inflows were concentrated in January, reflecting strong investor sentiments amid the rally in gold prices.

The investor interest in gold ETFs has been sustained and continues to broaden, albeit at a slower pace, as reflected in the number of accounts or folios of gold ETFs. March saw the addition of 0.31mn new accounts (vs 0.65mn in February), taking the total number to 12.39mn. Furthermore, gold ETFs’ share in the overall assets under management (AUM) of mutual funds has seen a rise to 2.3% from 0.9% a year ago.

Digital gold sees continued uptake

The purchases of digital gold via the Unified Payments Interface (UPI) remained strong in February, albeit below January’s record levels.8 Total purchases during the month totalled INR 30.3bn, equivalent to an estimated 1.9t (Chart 4) in volume terms, which is 53% higher than the average monthly volume of 1.2t over the preceding 13 months. Although digital gold purchases are currently unregulated, this channel continues to attract retail investors, likely driven by the ease of transactions and low minimum investment requirements. The value of these transactions has increased nearly fourfold over the past year, highlighting the growing need for regulatory oversight.

RBI holding onto gold reserves

Despite a 4% decline in RBI forex reserves to US$697bn over the five weeks to 3 April, its gold holdings have remained largely stable. The RBI added a marginal 0.17t to its gold holdings in the week ending 3 April, taking total gold reserves to 880.5t, accounting for 17% of overall forex reserves, up from 12% a year ago. Gold holdings have broadly remained steady since mid-2025, with the pace of purchases moderating in 2025 (4t) following significant additions in 2024 (73t).

Sharp decline in imports

Gold imports fell sharply in March 2026, declining 30% m/m and 59% y/y to US$3.1bn, a nine-month low. In volume terms, imports are estimated to be 20–25t, well below the 12-month average of 62t. The decline reflects a combination of demand softness and supply disruptions stemming from flight disruptions out of the Middle East, a key transit hub for bullion flows into India.

Footnotes

1 LBMA gold price PM and MCX spot gold price as of 13 April 2026.

2 Akshaya Tritiya- 19-20 April 2026.

3 LBMA gold price PM.

4 LBMA gold price PM and MCX spot gold price as of 13 April 2026.

5 Amendment in Import Policy of items covered under Chapter 71 of ITC (HS)2022, 02 April 2026

6 Titan Company, Senco Gold Ltd, Kalyan Jewellers India Ltd, PC Jeweller Ltd, PN Gadgil Jewellers Ltd.

7 AMFI reported March 2026 net inflows (~US$244mn) exceeds our US$177mn, estimated based on partial information.

8 Digital gold is a physical gold product that is purchased electronically by customers and held in professionally managed vaults until the customer chooses to sell the gold or take physical delivery. Providers of digital gold include payment applications, jewellers, and online investment platforms.

data.title: Global Wealth Research - April 2026
data.description:
By Indrani De, CFA, PRM, Head of Global Investment Research FTSE Russell, David McNay, CFA, Director - Global Investment Research FTSE Russell, and Zhaoyi Yang, CFA, FRM, Sr Manager - Global Investment Research FTSE Russell

Middle East conflict injects volatility into markets. Bonds and “new defensives” when sentiment is weak, but markets returning to secular themes on de-escalation narratives: AI infrastructure, governance reform and EM.

The conflict in Iran injecting uncertainty and raising stagflation risk

  • Iran isn’t a significant oil exporter, only producing about 1.7mbpd, circa. 90% of which goes to China. However, the Strait of Hormuz is critical for global supply with circa 25% of all seabound oil (~20mbpd) passing the Strait, mostly destined for Asia. The most affected countries in Asia are South Korea and Japan, who import >85% of their energy (Source: IEA); China and India are also significant net importers of energy. China is better insulated than other Asian economies with an estimated reserve of 1.1 to 1.4 billion barrels (approx. 120 days).
  • The most direct vector for the conflict into markets is via higher energy prices, which affect inflation. 2Y inflation swaps in the US and Europe have increased by 1% and 1.3%, respectively, since the end of February. Markets are moving to price out further central bank rate cuts; Fed Funds Futures were pricing 2 further cuts by December 2026, both of which were priced out in March. Similarly, the 2 cuts expected by the Bank of England (BOE) have been repriced as 1-2 hikes; the BoE warned 1M households already facing higher borrowing costs because of the war in Iran.
  • At what level of oil price does demand destruction impact the economy? It is difficult to measure for a variety of factors – but we can simplify the question (at least in the US) by considering what level of gasoline price causes the US consumer to cut back? Work by the Dallas Fed after the 2022 shock would imply somewhere between $4.50-$5.00 per gallon – if this level holds true, then we may expect more weakness from the consumer. This may in turn increase political pressure on the US administration on finding a resolution to the conflict.
  • The probability of stagflation scenarios has increased. Increased energy costs will drive inflation higher, and supply constraints reduce productivity, which will pressure growth. Some countries, like the Philippines and Pakistan, have reduced working weeks in some sectors due to energy supply risks. This is notable because disruptions in countries which feed into low-margin goods in the global supply chain are a risk that could in turn lead to supply disruptions of mostly lower-value goods downstream.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of April 7, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD unless noted otherwise. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Volatility has increased, but what is it telling us?

a. Different measures all point to elevated risk going forward.

  • Prior to the conflict starting, equity volatility was relatively muted: the Russell Volatility Index (RVX) was below the mean, and vol-of-vol was low. Downside protection from options was inexpensive relative to average pricing of the 10Y average risk reversal.
  • Energy markets were faster to react. Option skew started moving higher in January – analogous to higher premiums for insuring against higher oil prices. Importantly, despite the sharp increase in the oil price in March, skew is still high to the call side. This means that even with oil at >$100/barrel, markets are paying large premiums to protect against another jump higher in oil prices.
  • RVX has trended higher, rather than spiking. Distinction being that unlike April ’25 when RVX spiked to >45 on US tariff announcements and then normalized quickly, during the Iranian conflict RVX has gradually drifted to less extreme but more stable volatility levels. This may potentially be a precursor to a more protracted period of elevated volatility, more akin to 2022.
  • The volatility of Russell 2000 skew had increased, but overall, the market remained sanguine during the first few weeks of the conflict. Only towards the end of March did skew push to the put side – implying markets are paying to protect against drawdowns.

b. Option skew primer

  • Covered in more detail in: What markets are telling us about the duration of the Middle East conflict
  • Option skew gives us an indication of how much the market is willing to pay for downside vs upside protection using options. The more negative the line, the more markets are willing to pay to protect their portfolios against a downside shock.
  • For this we look at the 3M Δ20 risk-reversal, which is the call implied volatility minus the put implied volatility; remembering a call gives the holder the right to buy at a given strike price and the put option provides the right to sell at a given strike.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of March 31, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Conflict of commodity prices: risk premiums are elevated even if there is de-escalation

a. Oil markets are pricing in material supply disruption over the next 6M

  • Oil curves, measured by Brent futures, were flat at the start of 2026, but since the start of the conflict, they have moved into a deep backwardation – reflecting the supply shock from a functional closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The steepness of the curve implies the market anticipates normalization of supply-demand balance over the next 6M. This assumption is a key risk for markets.
  • The level of the curve is circa. $20/barrel higher, meaning that the whole curve is more expensive than the start of 2026. This can be interpreted as an additional risk premium, which is important. If it persists at current levels, that implies ~1/3 increase in the price of oil year-on-year, which will have an impact on inflation expectations even if there is rapid de-escalation in the Middle East.
  • Longer-dated deliveries have been well anchored even as cease-fire talks commence, supporting the idea risk premiums will persist.
  • Whilst energy is the primary vector, the conflict is causing distortions in other commodity markets. Aluminum has rallied >10% after Iranian strikes on aluminum processing plants have impacted global supply. Longer-term demand for “energy transition” commodities (like lithium, nickel, and rare-earth minerals) may see further tailwinds due to accelerating demand for oil alternatives.

b. Was gold a hedge? It did its job

  • Gold fell 12% in March, the largest fall in gold in 35 event studies we looked at back to 1980.This raises the question about the role of gold as a risk and inflation hedge.
  • Higher oil prices have driven real yields higher, raising the opportunity cost of holding gold as a non-yielding asset.
  • Importantly, as a risk hedge, gold served its purpose. FTSE CRB Gold posted ~24% over 3M prior to the conflict – with a 52W equity-gold correlation of circa 0.0.
  • Gold typically acts as a store of value over time that is decorrelated to equity. That is distinct from an inflation hedge which is an asset that goes up when inflation rises.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of April 8, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Fixed Income yield has become more attractive since the geopolitical shock, with higher nominal and real yields

  • Nominal yields increased substantially in March in response to the Middle East conflict, contrary to the usual safe-haven dynamics, as markets focused on risks of higher inflation and hawkish-biased monetary policy. It was only from March 30 onward that yields declined, as safe-haven demand for government bonds re-emerged amid growing concern for longer-term economic growth. This implies a shift in market focus from inflation to growth. Yields staying elevated, inflation risk dominating safe-haven buying, sell-offs in both equities and bonds, all these point to stagflationary pressures.
  • As a result of sharper increases in short-term yields (largely driven by higher inflation expectation), 20s/2s curves bear flattened substantially in March (except Japan), led by Germany and the UK. Despite March flattening, 20s/2s curve steepness remained above long-term averages, with Japan at 230 bps and US & UK at 115 bps. This dynamic reflects secular forces (a recovery in term premium from inverted curves post 2022 rate hikes, meaning investors once again being compensated for holding longer duration) being confronted by opposing forces upon Middle East conflict shock (short yields rose more than longs, driving curves flatter).
  • 7-10yr yield increases in March were driven more by inflation expectation (vs real yields) in the UK and Germany than in the US, as Europe and the UK are more vulnerable to the energy supply shock than the US given their heavier reliance on energy imports.
  • Real yields on inflation-linked bonds (World ILSI) increased 25 bps in 7-10yr, and 32 bps in 20yr+, through March. Latest valuations of linkers look compelling on a historical basis, with real yields on both 20yr+ (2.6%) and 7-10yr (1.8%) at about the 98th percentile across 10-year history. Demand for near-term inflation protection drove 1-3yr real yields lower in March, reflecting higher market-implied inflation expectation. Breakeven inflation (BI) curves are currently inverted, with higher BI rate in 1-3yr, as markets had priced for a short-term disruption.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of March 31, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD unless noted otherwise. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

From Risk-on-Risk-off to Themes-on-Themes-off: RORO to TOTO

a. Markets moving between ‘defensive’ and thematic

  • Markets have been shifting between ‘defensive’ positioning and the themes that were driving markets pre-Middle East conflict; on de-escalation, EM, Korea, and Japan have rebounded.
  • The US has been the top-performing key equity market since the start of the conflict. The US status as an energy exporter and large tech weight helped relative performance.
  • Chinese equities were supported by a resilient RMB which underpinned returns in dollar terms. China’s strategic oil reserves and alternative supply (like Russia) provided a buffer. Lack of foreign ownership may also help explain China’s outperformance (China under 5% vs around 33% in Japan and Korea).

b. Markets reframing ‘defensive’ as Tech, Utilities & Listed Infrastructure

  • Returns from traditional defensive industries (Consumer Staples, Healthcare, Utilities, and Telecoms) have been mixed since the start of the conflict in the Middle East, highlighting how investors need to be more selective when protecting portfolios.
  • Energy has been the only industry with positive returns; although energy equity returns have been modest relative to the underlying commodity prices implying markets still aren’t seeing the crisis as a significant earnings tailwind.
  • Technology, particularly mega-cap names, has fared relatively well which reflects a flight to quality. Companies with strong balance sheets, global revenue streams and high liquidity were favored as being better insulated.
  • Utilities did well relatively but remain in negative territory since the start of the conflict. Utilities benefit from their traditional defensive nature with inelastic demand curves and limited margin pressure, as well as tailwinds from AI demand. The same dynamics supported FTSE Global Listed Infrastructure, which was only down -2.0% (to April 7, 2026); bolstered by regulated utilities and AI.
  • Consumer Staples, a traditional defensive, is suffering from higher input costs and expected pressure on margins.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of April 7, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD. * South Korea is classified as a Developed market in FTSE equity indices, account for 2.5% of market cap within FTSE Developed Index. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Were traditional safe havens reliable during March?

  • Cross-asset performances and commodity returns saw even traditional safe havens like government bonds and gold decline during the March geopolitical conflict. Only the US dollar upheld its safe- haven role. In March, Commodities and listed Infrastructure (due to inflation-hedging characteristics) outperformed equities, in line with their historical outperformance in stagflationary environments. As a result of higher Commodities vs lower equities, Commodities relative performance vs equities rebounded strongly in March.
  • Commodities posted substantial returns of 19.5% in March, as crude oil price surged, alongside a rally in Aluminum price after attacks on production facilities. Oil price 1-month change of 63% ranks as one of the largest percentage moves in history. By contrast, precious and industrial metals including silver, gold, and copper, declined by 7.8-19.2% over 1M. This divergence within commodity sectors (higher oil vs weaker industrial metals such as copper, a growth indicator) may point to stagflationary risks.
  • Both equities and bonds saw sharp sell-offs in March, consistent with a stagflationary scenario, driving stock-bond correlation higher to 0.4, from -0.17 pre-conflict.
  • Like government bonds, gold did not perform as a reliable safe haven during the recent shock, down by 12% in March, likely due to its non-yielding nature and sensitivity to changes in real yields. Higher yields raise the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets such as gold. A stronger US dollar may also have weighed on global demand for gold, given its priced in dollars. Gold had already rallied strongly pre-Iran conflict (as had silver), so arguably it has served its purpose as a haven asset and now there is profit taking. Gold rebounded since Mar 31st as real yield eased.
  • The 2.4% appreciation of the US dollar in March (vs -4.1% over 12M) was supported by safe-haven inflows, the US’s position as a net energy exporter, and shifting expectations toward delayed rate cuts.
